skill.name: skill-market-publisher
skill.description: Publish, submit, and verify local skills across public skill marketplaces, directories, and registries. Use when an agent needs to take a skill folder or skill repository, confirm current submission paths, prepare cross-market metadata, run verified live adapters, or generate manual submission bundles for markets such as AgentSkill.sh, Skillstore, SkillNet, agent-skills.md, SkillsMD, SkillHub, SkillHQ, and related registries.
skill.summary: Automate skill publishing across 25+ AI marketplaces with inspection, planning, bundling, and verified live submission.
skill.author: zjsxply

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 661 patterns across 7 files, but AI evaluation confirms the overwhelming majority are false positives. All 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are triggered by 'md' substrings in marketplace domain names (SkillsMD, mdskills, etc. in URL strings), not actual cryptographic usage. All 'Ruby/shell backtick' findings in .md files are documentation code blocks, not executable code. Subprocess usage is limited to legitimate git commands, external CLI probing, and browser opening. Network requests target documented marketplace APIs for the skill's core publishing function. No malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or obfuscation was found. The skill's capability combination (network + subprocess + filesystem) is inherent to its publishing function and presents low risk when used as documented.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via subprocess
medium_findings.description: The skill uses subprocess.run for git commands ('git rev-parse'), external CLI probing (clawhub), and browser opening (xdg-open/open). All subprocess calls use controlled arguments derived from CLI flags or hardcoded values. The --clawhub-bin parameter accepts an arbitrary executable path with default 'clawhub', representing a minor injection surface if an attacker controls the CLI arguments.

medium_findings.title: HTTP Requests to External Marketplace APIs
medium_findings.description: The skill makes HTTP POST and GET requests to 10+ external marketplace APIs for skill submission. All URLs are hardcoded marketplace endpoints. The skill transmits user-provided metadata (author name, email, skill description, repository URL) to these third-party services as part of its core publishing function.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Marketplace URLs
low_findings.description: The skill contains 200+ hardcoded URLs pointing to external marketplace APIs, documentation pages, and submission endpoints. These are configuration necessary for the skill to function and do not represent exfiltration. All URLs target legitimate skill marketplace services.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Write via Bundle Output
low_findings.description: The bundle command writes submission files to a user-specified output directory. The --out-dir parameter is user-controlled. Documentation examples show /tmp usage but the actual path is determined by the operator.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill executes external processes via subprocess.run for git operations, CLI tool probing, and live publishing. Arguments are either hardcoded or derived from CLI flags with constrained values. No shell=True usage found. Risk is low but the pattern warrants awareness.

dangerous_patterns.title: urllib.request HTTP Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses urllib.request for HTTP GET (recon, fetching docs) and POST (marketplace submissions). All target URLs are hardcoded marketplace endpoints. User-Agent is set to a fixed value. No credential exfiltration pattern was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Frontmatter Parsing from Untrusted Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill parses YAML frontmatter from SKILL.md files using yaml.safe_load (safe parser). A fallback simple parser handles cases where PyYAML is not installed. Both parsers operate on local file content under the user's control.

content.user_title: Publish Skills to Multiple AI Marketplaces with One Command
content.value_statement: Managing submissions across many AI skill marketplaces is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill automates the entire publishing workflow from inspection and planning to verified submission across 25+ marketplaces.
actual_capabilities: Inspect skill directories to extract metadata and detect repository layouts
Generate market readiness plans for 25+ skill marketplaces with compatibility checks
Create submission bundles containing manifest, market plans, and per-market payloads
Submit skills to supported marketplaces via HTTP APIs or external CLI wrappers
Verify marketplace submission endpoints with automated recon and marker checks
Generate manual submission checklists and open browser pages for markets without APIs
limitations: Cannot bypass login or authentication requirements on individual marketplaces
Manual-web markets still require the operator to complete browser-based forms
Marketplace submission APIs and endpoints may change and require adapter updates
Most markets require a public GitHub repository URL as a prerequisite for submission

use_cases.title: Publish a New Skill to Multiple Markets
use_cases.description: A skill developer creates a new skill and wants to list it on every supported marketplace. They use inspect, plan, bundle, and publish commands to submit to all verified auto-publish targets in sequence.

use_cases.title: Bulk Publish Skills from a Monorepo
use_cases.description: A repository maintainer manages a collection of skills in one repository. They run the bundle command for each skill directory to prepare per-market payloads, then publish each skill individually to the appropriate markets.

use_cases.title: Verify Marketplace Endpoint Health
use_cases.description: A marketplace operator or skill publisher wants to confirm that submission endpoints are functional before publishing. They run the recon command to check expected markers on each marketplace page and identify broken adapters.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Skill Directory
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the skill at /path/to/my-skill and show me its metadata, repository layout, and license information.
prompt_templates.scenario: You just created a new skill and want to verify its structure before publishing

prompt_templates.title: Plan Market Submissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a market readiness plan for my skill at /path/to/my-skill with repository URL https://github.com/myorg/myskill. Tag it as automation and testing. Show me which markets I can publish to and what is missing.
prompt_templates.scenario: You want to understand which marketplaces are compatible with your skill and what inputs each requires

prompt_templates.title: Create a Full Submission Bundle
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a submission bundle for my skill at /path/to/my-skill with repo URL https://github.com/myorg/myskill, git ref main, version 0.1.0, author email me@example.com, and output directory /tmp/skill-bundle. Use the automation category where applicable.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need review-ready payloads for all supported markets before any live submission

prompt_templates.title: Publish to a Specific Market
prompt_templates.prompt: Publish my skill at /path/to/my-skill to AgentSkill.sh using repo URL https://github.com/myorg/myskill. Execute the live submission and then verify the result.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have reviewed the dry-run output and are ready to submit to a live marketplace

output_examples.input: Run the plan command for my skill with repo URL and tags
output_examples.output: The plan output shows a table with one row per marketplace. Each row includes the market name, current mode (auto-http, auto-cli, manual-web), status (verified, blocked, auth-required), missing inputs you need to provide, and layout warnings if your repository structure does not match the market expectations. Markets marked as verified with no missing inputs are ready for publishing.

output_examples.input: Run the bundle command for my skill
output_examples.output: The bundle creates a directory containing a manifest.json with normalized skill metadata, a market-plan.json with the full readiness matrix, per-market payload files for wrapped targets, and a manual submission notes file with step-by-step checklists for every market that requires browser-based submission.

output_examples.input: Publish my skill to a verified market with execute flag
output_examples.output: The publish command sends the prepared payload to the target marketplace API, receives the response, and displays the result. For auto-http markets, it shows the HTTP response status and body. For auto-cli markets, it shows the CLI exit code and output. The output includes suggestions for verifying the submission using the market-specific verification steps from the playbook.

best_practices: Always run inspect and plan before publishing to any market to confirm compatibility and identify missing inputs
Review the dry-run output carefully before adding --execute to perform a live submission
Keep submission IDs, PR numbers, and dated evidence in your task conversation, not inside the reusable skill folder
anti_patterns: Do not submit to markets marked as incompatible or blocked in the plan output without resolving the underlying issue
Do not skip the bundle step before live submission as it generates the review packet needed to verify correctness
Do not store sample submission logs, live URLs, or dated field evidence inside the skill directory

faq.question: How many marketplaces does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill tracks over 25 skill marketplaces with varying levels of support: verified auto-publish via HTTP or CLI, manual web submission with checklists, index-only discovery, and documented but not yet wrapped targets.

faq.question: Do I need API keys or accounts for all marketplaces?
faq.answer: No, many marketplaces accept public submissions without authentication. The plan command reports the authentication requirements for each market, so you can focus on the ones that do not require sign-up first.

faq.question: What happens during a dry run?
faq.answer: A dry run shows you the exact payload or command that would be submitted without making any live requests. This lets you review the data before executing. You add the --execute flag only when you are ready to submit.

faq.question: Can I publish multiple skills at once?
faq.answer: The skill publishes one skill folder at a time. If you have multiple skills, run the bundle command for each skill directory to prepare separate submission packets, then publish each one individually.

faq.question: What if a marketplace changes its submission process?
faq.answer: Run the recon command to re-verify marketplace endpoints. It fetches each market page and checks for expected markers. If markers are missing, the marketplace may have changed and the adapter needs updating.

faq.question: Is a public GitHub repository required?
faq.answer: Most markets require a public repository URL to verify and ingest your skill. If your source repository layout does not match what a market expects, the skill can help you create a publish mirror repository instead of reshaping your source.
